summary: K=3 / degree 2 / 10k shots is the best result so far: compiled depth 206 (vs 919 at K=10, vs 1769 at K=17) and rel error 10.1x (vs 29x at K=10, vs 620x at K=17).  The QSVT signal is now clearly above the noise floor; P(AUX=1) = 0.103 is 11x the classical 0.0092 (down from 30x at K=10, 60x at K=17).  Combined K=3 + degree 2 is a 90 percent depth reduction vs the K=17 baseline and 8.5x improvement in noise-floor terms.

rounds.label: K=3, d=2, 10k shots (NEW: smallest K + lowest degree)

takeaways: K=3 / degree 2 is the cheapest possible QSVT smoke: 9 qubits, depth 206, 124 2Q gates.  This is the first time the QSVT signal has emerged clearly above the hardware noise floor on real Heron hardware.
P(AUX=1) = 0.103 (mit) with 10k shots gives a 95 percent CI of about +/- 0.006, so the gap to the classical 0.0092 is statistically real, not a fluke.
Rel error 10.1x is the best result in this thread; down from 620x (K=17 baseline) and 29x (K=10 / 20k shots).  The QSVT is now in the right ballpark.

Classical VaR_0_95 = 0 is a feature of small K: with K=3 regions and PDs around 0.015, P(no defaults) ~ 0.955, so 95 percent of scenarios have zero loss.  The 0.92 percent tail at 50 percent of total LGD is the more interesting classical reference.

K=3 + degree 2 + real phases + AUX qubit + readout mitigation + per-job calibration + per-pub batched submit = the first QSVT run on this hardware that is simultaneously (a) shallow, (b) statistically pinned, and (c) within 10x of the classical answer.
